speaker: interviewee
text: --that's when they didn't have any wood to-- we needed somebody to bring us some wood. Because everything was cooked and-- by wood or by now-- no stoves or nothing. Everything was primitive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this broke, and that broke. And all of a sudden, he was always available. I just went to him. I said, can you help us? You know, this. And once the-- the bed, which was sitting where it was broken, he helped us to make. He was become-- he had-- when we worked together in the potato cellars, he always came over. And he said, have you enough lunch? He had his lunch. He said, can he-- can I-- can you share-- you can share with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, you know. So I gave him something, the [INAUDIBLE]. He gave me something. And he did-- nothing happened just for about three months or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned before the-- a second [NON-ENGLISH] that took place. Can you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's the-- yes. First, I wind up how it happened, you know, that somehow I got stuck with this man. Yes, he made-- he made himself available. Wherever I went, he was. And he made himself available. And we needed the help of a man. We were three women. We needed help of a good man. He was a good man. And then the-- something happened. I don't know what happened. After maybe six months or something, just before the second [NON-ENGLISH], I said, you know, might as well be together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to-- to-- to-- I-- in the meantime, I was thinking, you know, if I ever have a chance, I want to escape. I can't-- and out here, I'm sentenced to death! So I-- I-- I don't want to be led to the-- to the slaughter like a sheep, you know? But this time I was aware of what's going on, and I decided I'm not going to. But how do you do it without a man?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, he was a strong man-- physical, very able, handy with-- in-- in primitive conditions. He-- he had two years in the Polish army. He knew the-- the-- the area, because he was a-- a grain merchant before the war. So he dealed with a lot of peasants and all sorts of thing. He knew every-- he knew all the-- he came from the-- he was born in Naliboki, where the Pushcha was. So he knew every scrap of everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I says, if there is anything better to escape, to plan a escape, it is this man. So I told him, look. If you agree to-- to escape, we'll-- we come together. So he said, oh, you know, I-- I wasn't thinking about that. What happen to everybody is going to happen to me. I'm not better than my wife or my children, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you owe it to them-- even to them to escape. We've got-- he-- he didn't believe in it. But he was-- he had the strength, but I had the enthusiasm. He didn't believe in escape first-- at first. But I talked it-- him into to. Plus, we sort of-- the fact that we'll escape together, that we'll be together. Anyway, I went into his quarters. And they made room for me. And it was easier on my sister. [LAUGHS] I made it easier on my sisters, and we became a couple-- just like that. And then came-- I went to work in those-- not from the potato sellers. I went to work in the workshops. And then the workshops shifted to the courthouse. And my husband learned in the army saddle making-- a bit of it. And then I said, since he already-- we already-- me and my two sisters were already in the-- on the-- in the workshops-- might was well get him in too. So we-- he sat for exam-- like, not a exam, but the saddle makers tried him out-- whether he can do something. And he got into the thing. He got into there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we all sort of-- and they was working in the workshops, and-- which was in the courthouse-- situated in the courthouse. And at-- and we used to go home to the ghetto, always escorted to and from work by armed guards.

speaker: interviewee
text: We weren't allowed to-- to walk in the footpaths-- only in the middle of the road. And that's how we used to march in the morning, and march at night after work, to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they started to put wired-- barbed wire around the workshop where we worked. I said, what are they doing this for? One-- one fence, another fence, and coils in between-- and around the-- around the whole courthouse. I said, what is this for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very suspicious. And in the meantime, while I was working there, the director of the-- those workshops was my friend from the-- from my college! He was made to be a director.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he ask me. He said, you're not worrying that, you know, your-- one of your friends is at-- is at the school? School friends is a director. I-- I didn't-- don't really want to be a director. They made me, because I had threatened him.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is a director, so send him to the army, or something. But I helped. So he became. And I talked him into it. I said, you'd be good. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, they have a-- so the-- a knowledge of it, of what's going on behind the scene. He used to tell me. And the first thing he-- he came in, and into the-- when we-- she-- when they put all those barbed wires and everything. He had a list of the people who worked there, and he didn't find my name!

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he came into the-- he said, where-- you are not on the list. I said, yes, I am. And he said, no you're not. He showed me the list. Here on the top of it was Abramowicz. I say, I was no longer Rak, I was Abramowicz. [LAUGHS] That's why he thought I wasn't on the list. He didn't know. Oh, he said, you got married. I said, yes. He found out later, you know, how it all came about. He was really good to all the Jews. But some Jews weren't grateful, because they wanted to push out some people and get-- because everybody knew this is the most secure job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after they put all those barbed wire fences in, I think something was suspicious. So I asked Shkliarevski. And I said, what's going on? And he said, like in White Russian, [NON-ENGLISH]. It means a big cloud is gathering in there. But this place is going to be all right. So I understood that the ghetto is going to be liquidated. And another-- there was another group of people who worked in the-- it's-- what's it called? [NON-ENGLISH], in the military barracks-- one group of people. This group, he said, is going to be exempt, and the people from the thing. Everybody else will go. So everybody else started to come to him and getting-- and every-- the-- the news spread And everybody trying to get into the workshops, and trying to-- to push us out. Because me and my sister weren't any dressmakers. My sister was, but [LAUGHS] I wasn't. And my other sister wasn't. So I tried to give a-- a bribe to Shkliarevski, you know-- as you know. These are no dressmakers. So those skilled dressmakers are sitting there cleaning bricks, and you've got two people that can't even, you know, hold a needle.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could hold a needle. I did help my sister, back in Warsaw, even. And-- and I knew a bit about it, but I wasn't a-- a professional tradeswoman.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, thanks to Mr. Shkliarevski, we didn't get pushed out. As he said, well, you stay. I won't let you be pushed out. So otherwise, you know, we would have been pushed out of it-- this, because the pressure was big. So they could go to the Germans and say, there is some people who don't know what they-- they're only sitting there, because it's safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we stayed. And the second [NON-ENGLISH] took-- took place.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day in the August-- I can't remember whether it was the 8 or 7th, or something like that. And we were going to go home. As I said, stay. You're not going. You're staying here. You're sleeping in the-- in the building where you work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. And then it started. People started, who were-- who were at work and trying to get home to the ghetto. So I heard something was-- because you could always feel it, see it. You see, the people who arrive. You see those Lithuanians, you see those-- more-- more guards, more police, more everything. And so I knew that's going-- what's going to happen. So I start hiding in the-- in all sort of places. And some of them came to the-- to the gate of the courthouse, to the workshops. Actually, Mr. Shkliarevski opened the gate and let quite a few people in. And that was it. We stayed there. And the next day, there was an execution. And everybody what got-- the second round got wiped out, and that. But this time, it wasn't just the-- the people from Nowogrodek, but from-- they brought in from the smaller places around it. From the small villages, and-- and townships, they brought them in and things. So it's another, probably 4,000 went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone escape from the ghetto at this-- at this stage?

speaker: interviewee
text: At this stage-- before this stage, some people went-- had connections with peasants and had a lot of money. They-- they tried to get-- a few. A few went out, yeah. But not-- not as-- not organized. It was all done privately with arrangements, with-- with some people. Some people got together with the peasants and gave them money. And-- because they were afraid that if they survived, they-- they might want their money back. It didn't work sometimes. It was their last thing to do-- to give the peasants money for-- for safekeeping-- for keeping them safe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much longer did you stay after this-- after the second [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: After this, after the second [NON-ENGLISH], there was about 500 of us. And then they brought from remnants of the [NON-ENGLISH] ghetto, and-- and from other camp-- I think from [NON-ENGLISH] from somewhere else, a few more people. And those people, for-- I think, mostly from [NON-ENGLISH]. Because I lived to-- close to Pushcha, which is a huge forest. This was in [NON-ENGLISH], Pushcha. And so I had some connections. I knew something from existence of partisans. And they tried to-- to escape. One group was caught. And-- and they were shot on the spot and left lying there. So the next day, everybody can see them. And they-- they confiscated their ration of bread for the-- for a week. It was 200 when we were actually living in the ghetto, and this was the-- the separated the work of-- of the workshops. And the living quarters, was in the one. This-- this was established as a-- as a self-contained ghetto-- the tradespeople . And that one was still there. Some-- some people were still in the other ghetto left-- the one that worked in the military barracks, and a few who had managed to survive. They were there, in the other ghetto. And the rest, and they-- and they brought in some-- also some more from the villages and-- and small townships to fill it up. And because I looked for that-- them-- the smaller ones. And I brought them here so it would be easier to get them together. And we saw that people escaped from-- the other ghetto's people start escaping through the-- because the other people were going out to work. So they'd work this one. So they made contacts while on their way to work or sometime-- sometimes. If they were caught, they were shot on the spot. It doesn't matter, because they tried. But in this ghetto-- and it was-- by then it was called the [NON-ENGLISH], Nowogrodek-- [NON-ENGLISH]. It means a works camp. [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], they called it in German. And we saw those people from [NON-ENGLISH] trying to escape. And some of them succeeded. As I said, there was a whole lot of people there in the Pushcha. But it wasn't our side. It was the other side of the Pushcha. We didn't go to this Pushcha. And so the idea went into our heads. We would have to try to escape, because there's not-- not many of us left. And we will be the next victims-- next on the list-- no-- not many more to choose from. So I start asking my husband. I said, let's see how we can do it. In the meantime, people are coming in, like the [NON-ENGLISH], were for-- for the local populations. They came, and they got the boot makers, their dressmakers, their tailors. They brought-- brought some work. And they paid. And the German government was making money. We did-- we only got 250 grams of bread and a-- a ladle full of thin soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: And usually, we traded. So everybody traded everything else at the sale. The Christian people were so used to Jews being killed. And if someone wore a watch, or a ring, or something, they said, what do you need it for? You're going to die. Selling it to his-- you can eat for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, oh, this is from-- this is from my mother. This is from my-- doesn't matter! You know, she-- you-- it's going-- you're not going. That's how they talked to us. And my sister had very good connections, because she was the one that was admitting the clients, and taking the measurements, and discussing the fashion what they want. So they used to bring food. So we ate. And other people had possessions they couldn't-- they didn't have access to the-- so they gave her things to sell. So she had a bit of commission, you know. If somebody got a-- a kilo of butter, they gave a piece of butter. They gave a piece of this. So, you know. So-- so we-- we ate. So we weren't really hungry in this [NON-ENGLISH]. We-- we had food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And other people had food by selling whatever is left of-- everybody always had something hidden, you know? They're just selling the last cow to the last pair of shoes, for those more important.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then a messenger came in town, you know, not-- somebody called Kozlovski, as I told you before. And in the Russian language, you call-- it was called [RUSSIAN]. It means somebody you can trust-- who you can-- so-- so I picked him out. And he came. And he says-- he said, there is a group of partisan organized. And there's-- they're called Bielski. And they would like people from ghetto to join them. All you have to do is to get out. And he-- he told us, of course, in confidence. He didn't tell it to anybody that-- he told people in confidence where to come to. There was a-- a road what-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH], and the route to [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was a-- what's-- in Russian, they called it a [RUSSIAN]. In English, it's like a hamlet. And he lives there. That's the-- I think, the 16th kilometer-- the 13th. I can't remember what that said-- in the 16th kilometer on the way-- the only one. You can't miss it. And come to this place. And we will put you in contact with the-- with the Bielskis, with the partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after I've heard this, it was no question. We're going to run. [LAUGHS] We-- we've got somewhere to go. We can't stay here any longer. So I start agitating my husband. And he organized a group. He was very well, sort of-- everybody wanted to be in his group. But he selected ones-- the useful ones. [LAUGHS] How many of there were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were 20-- 12, sorry-- 12 people. Actually, we were supposed to be 10, but one was-- one was sort of-- there was a couple who had a-- a-- a boy, about a 12-year-old boy. We're not supposed to take boys to this, but I sort of-- because I have hidden him. And when we were-- came out, he-- he was there. And another lady decided, very wisely, she said, once they go, I'll go with them. I don't care whether I'm in the group or not. [LAUGHS] And she came!

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your sisters with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters-- my husband says he can't take three women. I said, we'll go, and-- and we'll have a look what's going on. And we'll come back. You-- people-- we'll come back and get you. Because that-- that why the-- the was the-- why it was working. One went out and and then came, and back-- and came back. And you took the-- sometimes see, it was-- it wasn't successful. Sometimes it was successful, at this stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, we'll-- we'll send for you. We'll come for you. My husband promised them that he'll-- that he'll come and take them back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we organized this group-- very good, well-- well-organized, very useful people. All-- one of them was a locksmith. And one he's a-- he built the-- the gates. He was very clever. And he had made a key, a spare key to the gate. And so we had one with key. Two of them were builders, which was good for-- my husband was military. And also he-- he was very handy with everything. He knew the way of every corner of the-- how to go, with the-- with the sides-- sideways and through the fields. He know-- knew the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then there was another couple. And there were how many women? There was supposed to be, originally, three women and seven men. It finished up with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] four women, and seven men, and one boy. So that's-- that's-- that's how it finished with 12-- 2 boys, actually. This other one had a son, but he had-- he didn't have a wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had to organize it very accurately. It had-- the whole escape had to take place in five minutes. So we had to be very ready. Because at five minutes-- I'd say, like, it was light autumn, like November-- and very dark. And we had to-- well, I had to-- I had to go before the snow really settles in-- not to leave footprints. And we went-- we all-- we decided that two people-- two-- the two groups-- divided in two groups. One group will go through the gate, which this man will open. The other-- because 12 people or 10 people at the same time, it's too much. It can be easily noticed. And the other ones will go-- they already had something worked out, bit by bit every day, at one spot in the-- in the wires, where you can go through with some help from trusted friends. They helped you. They picked it up, the wires, and you-- we worked on it every day, a little bit, sort of. We knew which pocket-- which point you can cross. And another one was to distract the-- the guards. And another very important was that it was-- at 5 o'clock every night, the guards changed. Every evening, the guards changed. By the time they went in, and the other ones came back, there was a little distraction. And, you know, they didn't just come and take over. But they went in this little room. It was a-- it was a-- it went away. And when they came in, the other one went out. Five minute elapsed. This five minutes, it had to-- had to be done. And somebody came from the-- from the-- they knew it were-- there was is group trying to escape. As I said, you've got five minutes. Open the door and go. So we opened the door. I went through the gate. My husband has already escaped through the wires. He was already there. The wires went first, because that could take longer. And we went to the-- through the gates-- the women and the children, you know. And the more able ones went through the wires. About a 1/4 kilometer or something, 200 meters, was a little forest. We will all meet there. We all-- we were all there, and came there. We all meet there. Everything went smoothly. And when it happened, this little-- and then we started on our way to the-- Mr. Kozlovski, to the-- to the hamlet, which connected us with the partisan.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came. We arrived safely-- very, very tiring, and very frightened. We sort of-- every-- every sound, we thought they are-- they are after us-- every-- you know? It was a very still, quiet night. And there was nobody in the roads.

speaker: interviewee
text: And bit by bit, we arrived. We were very tired, and very hungry, and very cold. And we get-- and we got some food. And we got some very nice reception.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you all right? You arrived-- you arrived to-- to the right place. He-- he made us sit in one-- it's in-- in those peasants' huts-- houses. They have those big ovens. You sleep on them. You know? This-- so we went to sleep on the oven-- [LAUGHS] on the oven, where it was warm. He made us a hot meal. In the morning, he said, you know, I can't-- nobody will come now, because it's night. I'll take you to a little-- a-- a-- wooden-- wooded place where you can sit all day till the-- till it gets dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because at-- at-- at daytime, Germans can come, and, you know. At night, they don't go. But in-- in daytime, stay off the known roads. So he put us in-- in this little forest, and we waited all day. And then in the evening, when it was dark, as he promised, he came and took us into his house. And not long afterwards came the Bielskis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the Bielskis?

speaker: interviewee
text: The three brothers came in. All of a sudden, I say-- I say-- I heard a noise. And horses, I hear-- big horses. And three-- three huge men, dressed in like, military black, with sub-machine guns-- short machine guns, they used to call it. [RUSSIAN], in Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I sat. And I just said-- and I says, who's that? He said, those are the guys. They're coming to-- to collect us, or something-- to kill us or something, I said. I said, that's the-- that's the Bielski brothers. Here they are. They came to fetch you to the partisans.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's just absolutely unbelievable. From being this-- in one night and one day, from being this little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] suffering, this-- this-- little, obedient, this little despised person, all of a sudden, I'm in-- in the presence of Jews with arms, with-- it's just-- in one-- in one day! It was-- this is most incredible feeling! And [SIGHS] as I said, they went to the-- to him, to the-- Mr. Kozlovski. And I said, look. I'll tell you where to take them to. Even-- even to them, to him, I said, I didn't tell them the right-- the right location of the base-- not like it's written in the books that he took them to the base. Kozlovski, it's-- he did not take anybody to the base.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then-- it's that-- they trusted him, but just in case he gets caught, you know? He can tell where it was. He didn't. He didn't-- he didn't take us to the base, Kozlovski. He took us to a certain spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, you spend the night here. And in the morning, he-- they'll come and they'll pick you up. They know where you are, the Bielskis.

speaker: interviewee
text: So try and make a fire, warm yourself up, otherwise, you'll freeze to death. It was very hard to make a fire, but eventually, we succeeded. And my husband took off a sock and then bound the sock. The-- the matches got-- got wet, you know? [LAUGHS] And the wood was wet, but somehow they managed. They were very resourceful men. Good to be in company of strong, resourceful men in the jungle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they made a fire. And we warmed ourself. We spent the night there. Nobody slept, of course. And first thing in the morning, as they said, they came and said, come. Let's go to the base.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came to get you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- some of the Bielskis, yeah. I can't remember who it was, but the Bielskis, or somebody, some partisans, I don't know. And they came. And it turned out we were about 10 meters from the-- [LAUGHS] the base-- not-- not far-- just around. it went through some-- some bushes, and all of a sudden, we came into a camp-- incredible. Everybody was sitting by a fire. Everybody is act-- is busy doing something. It's-- it was this incredible feeling. All of a sudden, I've changed from a prisoner to a-- a free person. And it's-- you just long so much for freedom when you are-- when you are thing-- when you are jailed.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember just going out for water was, for me, I was just sitting down near this-- a little river where we took out the water from, you know? And I said, ooh. And-- and I saw the nature, and the sky, and everything, and-- and the birds, and everything. I said, gee. It's nice to be free, even for-- for 15 minutes. And it was such a horrible feeling to go back to the ghetto. But, you know. And all of a sudden, I'm free, you know? It's just an-- this unbelievable feeling; and other-- other-- other sort of Jews, not-- not the one-- the one in the thing-- in the prisons. How many people were there, about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't count them. But it was quite a big base-- but not-- someone-- one told them the base. Someone-- there was a big spread, you know? Someone went on missions. Someone-- I couldn't really count. But afterwards, I counted about-- when we came into-- to the winter base. I'll tell you about it later. Not-- not long afterwards, we went to a winter base. This was just temporary bases. We didn't stay long in one place. We just wandered around, because they-- they weren't safe, because they were small forests. And-- but it sort of-- and I couldn't count them, no. But later on, I thought, I think 150 or something-- and something like that-- 100 and-- but the amount-- so [NON-ENGLISH] is about 30 in 1. And some people were not sitting-- so we were sort of doing other things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you meet-- you met the Bielski brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Right-- I met them, the ones that came to pick-- to-- to meet us at the-- at this place, at this hamlet. It was a Christian's place, Mr. Kozlovski's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us-- you-- you've got to pay him a little bit of money, because he-- for all of his expenses. He goes to town. He fetches this. He fetches for us that, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he risk his life for everybody-- gratitude money, you know? And he says, we were prepared for this, you know, and-- and then, you know, we were just-- that-- that was the three Bielskis-- the three Bielski brothers, yeah. Was there a leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was the one who introduced him. So we could recognize the leader right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It's just something about this man. He was a leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Tevia Bielski. He had-- we called him Tevia. He calls himself Tuvia, I know. It may be the Hebrew. And we called him Tevia, like the-- the one from the [LAUGHS] Tevia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like Tuvia, but instead of a U, we wrote an E. He would called him E-- Tevia. But he-- he changed his name later to Tuvia. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did--

speaker: interviewee
text: But in [NON-ENGLISH], we called him Tevia-- or the commandeer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: They looked terrific. They looked incredible-- the whole Bielski brothers-- the whole-- everybody looked terrific. Everybody was sort of-- there was a different-- one can tell different-- absolutely different atmospheres-- it's just incredible-- in one day.

speaker: interviewee
text: And because we were already-- or how many times-- how many years? Two years, I think, we spent before we escaped in the-- under the Germans. Thanks.
